g.data-narration: Welcome to Planning Graphs - a powerful AI planning technique that revolutionizes how we solve complex planning problems.
Traditional planning faces challenges - searching through huge state spaces is computationally expensive and inefficient.
g.text: Traditional planning searches through entire state space, which becomes
exponentially large with more variables. We need a more efficient approach!

g.data-narration: Planning graphs provide an elegant solution - a bipartite graph that captures state-action progression with efficient pruning.
g.text: A directed bipartite graph alternating between
• Proposition Levels (Pi) - literals that could be true

g.data-narration: Let's understand the structure: propositions at level 0 are initial state literals, then actions that can execute, then resulting states.
Proposition levels contain literals that could be true at that planning step, along with mutex links for incompatible propositions.
g.text: • Contains literals that could be true after i actions
• Includes all add-effects from previous action level
• Also includes persistent goals - propositions from Pi-1 not deleted

g.data-narration: Action levels contain all ground actions whose preconditions are satisfied in the previous proposition level.
g.text: • Contains applicable actions - those with preconditions satisfied in Pi-1
• Each action has: Preconditions (what it needs) and Effects (what it changes)
• Effects can be add-list (positive) or delete-list (negative)

g.data-narration: Mutex links show mutual exclusion - two propositions are mutex if they cannot both be true simultaneously in any valid state.
g.text: Two propositions are mutually exclusive if:
1. One is the negation of the other (e.g., On(A,B) vs ¬On(A,B))
2. All ways to achieve them involve mutex actions

g.data-narration: Two actions are mutex if they interfere with each other - conflicting effects, interference, or competing preconditions.
g.text: Two actions are mutex if any of these conditions hold:
1. Conflicting effects: One deletes what the other adds
2. Interference: One deletes a precondition of the other
3. Competing needs: Their preconditions are mutex at Pi-1

g.data-narration: In the expansion phase, GraphPlan builds the planning graph forward from the initial state, adding levels until goal appears or graph stabilizes.
g.text: Algorithm builds the graph level by level:
1. Start with P0 containing initial state literals
2. Add A1 with all applicable actions (preconditions in P0)
3. Add P1 with all effects from A1
4. Continue until goal appears OR graph stabilizes (no new propositions)

g.data-narration: In the extraction phase, GraphPlan searches backward from the goal, selecting non-mutex actions that achieve subgoals at each level.
g.text: Backward search to extract solution plan:
1. Start at goal level Pn where goal appears without mutex
2. Select actions from An that achieve goal subgoals
3. Ensure selected actions are not mutex with each other
4. Repeat backward until reaching initial state

g.data-narration: Solution extraction uses backtracking - if forward search fails, GraphPlan may expand the graph further and retry.
g.text: The extraction algorithm handles failure gracefully:
• If backward search fails at level n, try alternative action selections
• If no solution found, expand graph to level n+1
• Use backtracking when choices lead to dead ends

g.data-narration: Let's apply these concepts to a classic blocks world problem - three blocks stacked, goal is different arrangement.

g.text: A on B, B on C, C on table
A on B, B on C

g.data-narration: For our block world, we build levels - P0 has initial facts, A1 has applicable actions, P1 has effects, until goal appears.
g.text: Continue expanding until goal appears in a proposition level

g.data-narration: Planning graphs offer key advantages - efficient pruning through mutex links, avoids redundant computation, and provides completeness guarantees.
Planning graphs have limitations - cannot handle partial observability, uncertainty, or continuous time. They're for classical planning only.
Let's recap - planning graphs are bipartite structures with alternating proposition and action levels, with mutex links enabling efficient pruning.

g.text: • Planning Graph: Bipartite graph P0 → A1 → P1 → A2 → ...
• Mutex Links: Incompatibility at both proposition and action levels
• GraphPlan: Two phases - expand forward, extract backward
• Termination: Goal achieved OR graph stabilizes

g.data-narration: For your exam - remember the bipartite structure, know mutex conditions, and understand both phases of GraphPlan. Good luck!

g.text: 1. Remember: proposition and action levels always alternate
2. Know mutex conditions: negation, conflicts, interference, competing needs
